title: Bart Vs Australia

jokes.text: Go, toothpaste. Go! Move your pasty, white butt! Come on, shampoo! You can do it!
jokes.setup: Bart is apparently racing bathroom products
jokes.punchline: The over-the-top sports announcer enthusiasm for mundane bathroom items
jokes.explanation: Strong character moment that's perfectly Bart - finding entertainment in the mundane with inappropriate intensity

dialogue.speaker: Sports Announcer
dialogue.line: And it's down to the wire, folks! Charmin ultra soft taking the inside lane!

dialogue.speaker: Sports Announcer
dialogue.line: But wait, Cottonelle is making a bold move on the straightaway!

dialogue.speaker: Sports Announcer
dialogue.line: It's neck and neck! The tension is unbearable!

dialogue.speaker: Sports Announcer
dialogue.line: And the crowd goes wild as Bounty paper towels emerge from the curve!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Cowabunga!

jokes.text: No fair. You only won because you had the inside track.
jokes.setup: Bart's victory in his bathroom product race
jokes.punchline: Lisa's serious analysis of a completely ridiculous competition
jokes.explanation: Classic Lisa - applying logic and fairness to something completely absurd

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, I've run the numbers, and Bart's bathroom product actually has a 40% better glide ratio than the competition.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The aerodynamic properties are quite remarkable for something made entirely of soap.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I suppose even in the most frivolous competitions, science prevails.

jokes.text: It goes where it wants. Like me, babe.
jokes.setup: Scientific discussion about water drainage
jokes.punchline: Bart's pseudo-cool comparison of himself to water
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart attitude - rebellious and nonsensical with misplaced confidence

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I'm like water, man. I just flow around obstacles and end up in the lowest place possible.

jokes.text: I'm singing in the show... Hot! Cold! Hot! Cold! Hot! Who's doing that?
jokes.setup: Homer singing peacefully in the shower
jokes.punchline: Someone is clearly messing with the water temperature mid-song
jokes.explanation: Standard slapstick but well-executed with Homer's confused reaction

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ah, ah, ah, ah, ahhhh...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Ah, faithful toilet. You'll prove me right.
jokes.setup: Bart needs to prove water can spin the other way
jokes.punchline: Addressing toilet like a trusted ally in his scientific quest
jokes.explanation: Bart's relationship with inanimate objects as allies shows his childish logic and determination

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Okay, toilet. I know we've had our differences, but I need your help with something important.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I need to prove that water can spin the other way down here in the Southern Hemisphere.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So when I flush, I need you to spin counterclockwise. Can you do that for me, buddy?

jokes.text: So down there in, say, Argentina... or Rand McNally, all their water runs backwards?
jokes.setup: Lisa explaining the Southern Hemisphere
jokes.punchline: Homer casually lists the map company Rand McNally as if it's a country
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer stupidity - treating a map company as a geographical location with complete sincerity

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The Southern Hemisphere is the half of Earth south of the equator.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, you mean like Rand McNally?

jokes.text: In fact, in Rand McNally, they wear hats on their feet... and hamburgers eat people.
jokes.setup: Homer's confusion about Rand McNally being a place
jokes.punchline: Lisa plays along with increasingly absurd 'facts' about this fictional country
jokes.explanation: Classic escalation joke with perfect absurdist logic and great quotable imagery

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Rand McNally? That's a place? I thought it was just a map company.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, Rand McNally is a country. It's where they wear hats on their feet and hamburgers eat people.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, hamburgers eating people? That sounds dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: In Rand McNally, they drive on the left side of the right side of the road.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The left side of the right side? That makes perfect sense!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: And their national bird is the loon.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A loon? I love loons!

jokes.text: Cool.
jokes.setup: Lisa's absurd description of Rand McNally
jokes.punchline: Bart's completely unfazed acceptance of hamburgers eating people
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart reaction - maximum economy with one word that shows his skewed priorities

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: In Rand McNally, they wear hats on their feet and hamburgers eat people.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. Hamburgers eat people?

jokes.text: Please to repeat again, and I will translating for the el presidente.
jokes.setup: Bart calling random international numbers
jokes.punchline: Broken English response suggesting he reached some official who needs translation
jokes.explanation: Mild stereotypical humor that works in context but isn't particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hello? Hello, can anybody hear me?

dialogue.speaker: Foreign Official
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, I am here. I am... how you say... the Minister of—

dialogue.speaker: Foreign Official
dialogue.line: You must speak slowly. My English is not so good.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, yeah, hi. What country is this?

dialogue.speaker: Foreign Official
dialogue.line: This is very important government building. You call at bad time. We have crisis. Need translator very bad.

jokes.text: I can't get a straight answer out of this crazy hemisphere.
jokes.setup: Bart's frustration with international calls
jokes.punchline: Treating an entire hemisphere as if it's one uncooperative entity
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist logic - Bart's impatience with geography itself

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Why do international calls always have to be so complicated? It's like the entire Southern Hemisphere is conspiring against me!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's because it is, son.

jokes.text: Yes, this is Dr. Bart Simpson of the International Drainage Commission. It's an emergency.
jokes.setup: Bart making a collect call
jokes.punchline: His elaborate fake identity to get the call accepted
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart con artistry with appropriately official-sounding fake organization

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hi, I'd like to make a collect call please.

dialogue.speaker: Operator
dialogue.line: What name should I put it under?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh... Ivana Tinkle.

jokes.text: We understand drains in your area have malfunctioned... sucking in people and whatnot.
jokes.setup: Bart's fake emergency call premise
jokes.punchline: The casual 'and whatnot' after describing people being sucked down drains
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with perfect casual delivery of horrific scenario

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, hello, is this the Springfield Police Department? Yeah, I'm calling to report an emergency. There's people being sucked down the drains and whatnot.

jokes.text: Hey, Bart, the bakery caught fire and all of downtown smells like cookies. Want to go smell?
jokes.setup: Milhouse arriving to get Bart
jokes.punchline: The innocent excitement about disaster creating pleasant smells
jokes.explanation: Perfect kid logic - disaster as entertainment opportunity with innocent enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Hey Bart, what's that smell?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's the sewage treatment plant. It exploded.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Wow, cool! It smells like... popcorn!

jokes.text: Yes. Yes, I do.
jokes.setup: Milhouse's invitation to smell the fire
jokes.punchline: Bart's immediate abandonment of his phone scam for this opportunity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart priorities - immediately distracted by new mischief opportunity

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Hey Bart, come smell the fire!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Gotta go, I'll call you back.

jokes.text: Nine hundred dollary-doos?!
jokes.setup: Australian man seeing his phone bill
jokes.punchline: Australian slang version of 'dollars' that sounds childish
jokes.explanation: Playful Australian stereotype that became quotable despite being somewhat lazy

dialogue.speaker: Bruno
dialogue.line: Crikey! Look at this phone bill! That's gonna cost me fifty dollarydoos!

jokes.text: Who do they think I am, some stupid Aussie drongo?
jokes.setup: Bruno being transferred multiple times
jokes.punchline: His indignation while using distinctly Australian slang that proves the stereotype
jokes.explanation: Nice irony as he denies being stereotypically Australian while using Australian slang

dialogue.speaker: Bruno
dialogue.line: G'day mate! You're transferring me again? That's not fair, ya drongo! I've been nothin' but a good bloke here, and this is how ya treat me? Stone the crows!

jokes.text: Hey, I think I hear a dingo eating your baby.
jokes.setup: Bruno threatening Bart over the phone
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual reference to famous Australian tragedy/meme
jokes.explanation: Dark but effective - Bart using pop culture reference as weapon, perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Bruno
dialogue.line: Listen here, Simpson. You better watch your back.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, whatever. That's just like, drop bears and the guy who lost his arm to a crocodile, man.

jokes.text: Hey, Gus! I got something to report to you!
jokes.setup: Bruno going to his member of Parliament
jokes.punchline: Implied visual of very casual, informal political system
jokes.explanation: Solid visual gag about Australian informality in government

dialogue.speaker: Bruno
dialogue.line: I'm going to see my member of Parliament about this.

dialogue.speaker: Member of Parliament
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, come in, come in. Just move those empty beer cans off the couch.

jokes.text: Aye, mates. What's the good word?
jokes.setup: Introduction to Australian Prime Minister
jokes.punchline: Extremely casual greeting from head of government
jokes.explanation: Completes the escalation of Australian informality stereotype

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: G'day mate!

jokes.text: Look at this country. 'You-are-gay.'
jokes.setup: Homer reading 'Uruguay' on the map
jokes.punchline: His mispronunciation making it sound like an insult
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer juvenile humor - finding accidental insults in country names

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're a gay!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's Uruguay.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Eh, close enough.

jokes.text: Undersecretary for International Protocol, Brat and Punk Division.
jokes.setup: State Department official introducing himself
jokes.punchline: Absurdly specific government division for dealing with problem children
jokes.explanation: Great bureaucratic absurdity - government having specialized division for this exact problem

dialogue.speaker: Evan Conover
dialogue.line: Hello, I'm Evan Conover, Director of the Department of Cartography and Troublesome Children.

jokes.text: I know those words, but that sign makes no sense.
jokes.setup: Homer looking at some Australian cultural reference
jokes.punchline: His confused reaction to foreign culture/language
jokes.explanation: Good Homer confusion - understanding individual words but not meaning

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What the—? I can't understand a word of this!

jokes.text: That's tough but fair. Boy, go with the man.
jokes.setup: State Department wanting to imprison Bart for five years
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate willingness to sacrifice his son
jokes.explanation: Dark but perfectly Homer - casual willingness to abandon parental responsibility

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Five years?! That's not even a real punishment! I'd throw in an extra ten years just to get him out of the house!

jokes.text: The prison train is sailing.
jokes.setup: Trying to convince Homer about prison
jokes.punchline: Mixing transportation metaphors in a nonsensical way
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist humor mixing train and boat metaphors

dialogue.speaker: Conover
dialogue.line: Homer, prison isn't so bad. It's like a boat.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A boat?

dialogue.speaker: Conover
dialogue.line: Yes, and you're the captain... of a ship... that flies.

jokes.text: I can handle that. I'm an expert at phony apologies.
jokes.setup: Learning he just needs to make a public apology
jokes.punchline: Bart's proud admission of his insincerity skills
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart self-awareness - taking pride in his manipulation skills

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: An apology? That's easy. I'm the best liar in Springfield!

jokes.text: To combat homesickness, we have a device that makes them swirl the correct American way.
jokes.setup: Bart asking about toilet drainage at embassy
jokes.punchline: Elaborate technological solution to maintain American toilet behavior
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that escalates the original premise to absurd diplomatic lengths

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: So how does the toilet drainage work here at the embassy?

dialogue.speaker: Ambassador Ward
dialogue.line: Well, we have an elaborate system of pumps and filters to maintain proper American toilet behavior. You see, the local plumbing can't handle the American standard, so we had to install a special mechanism that converts our waste into a form compatible with their infrastructure.

jokes.text: Really? Look, boy. Now I'm in Australia. Now I'm in America. Australia. America.
jokes.setup: Learning about embassy soil rules
jokes.punchline: Homer repeatedly stepping back and forth across the boundary
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer physical comedy - childish fascination with arbitrary boundaries

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm on embassy soil! I'm not on embassy soil! I'm on embassy soil! I'm not on embassy soil!

jokes.text: Here in America, we don't tolerate that crap, sir.
jokes.setup: Homer's boundary-hopping antics
jokes.punchline: Official stopping the behavior that's perfectly legal
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to Homer's boundary game with authority figure intervention

dialogue.speaker: Marine Guard
dialogue.line: Sir, you're going to have to stop. You're on a naval base.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I'm not doing anything illegal!

dialogue.speaker: Marine Guard
dialogue.line: I know. That's what makes it so irritating.

jokes.text: You call that a knife? This is a knife. That's not a knife. That's a spoon. All right, all right. You win. I see you've played knifie-spoonie before.
jokes.setup: Classic Crocodile Dundee movie reference setup
jokes.punchline: Character holding a spoon, creating absurd made-up game
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of famous movie scene with absurd 'knifie-spoonie' game that became iconic

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: That's not a knife... that's a knife.

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: That's not a knife either, man. THIS is a spoon!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: A spoon? What are you gonna do, eat him to death?

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: Nah, I just made up this awesome game called Spoon Fighting. I'm gonna be rich!

jokes.text: Something wrong, Yank? No. It's pretty big, I guess.
jokes.setup: Homer ordering famous Australian giant beer
jokes.punchline: Implied visual of comically oversized beer that's underwhelming
jokes.explanation: Solid visual gag subverting expectations about Australian beer size

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll have one of those famous Australian giant beers.

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: That'll be $3.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Three dollars? That's a bargain!

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: Here you go.

jokes.text: I'll just have a cup of coffee. Beer it is. No, I said, coffee. Beer? Coffee. Beer? C-O... B-E...
jokes.setup: Homer trying to order coffee
jokes.punchline: Bartender's inability to comprehend anything but beer orders
jokes.explanation: Great escalating communication breakdown with perfect rhythm

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Could I get a coffee, please?

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: A coffee? I'm sorry, I don't understand. Did you say a Bud Light?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, coffee. Black coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: A black... Guinness? Coming right up.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, no! Coffee! The hot brown liquid!

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: Oh! You want a hot brown liquid? We have Schlitz!

jokes.text: What kind of a sick country would kick someone with a giant boot?
jokes.setup: Learning about the booting punishment
jokes.punchline: Homer's moral outrage at the specific implement while missing the broader point
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic - focusing on the wrong aspect of punishment

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You're gonna kick me with a boot? A BOOT?! Why not just use your foot like a normal person?!

jokes.text: Mr. Simpson, shush. Disparaging the boot is a bootable offense.
jokes.setup: Homer criticizing the boot punishment
jokes.punchline: Self-referential legal system where criticizing punishment earns that punishment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant recursive legal logic that became highly quotable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What kind of cruel and unusual punishment is this boot?

dialogue.speaker: Judge Conover
dialogue.line: Oh, you think that's cruel? Criticizing the boot earns you the boot.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But that doesn't make any sense!

dialogue.speaker: Judge Conover
dialogue.line: Criticizing the logic earns you the boot as well.

jokes.text: In America, we stopped using corporal punishment. And things have never been better. The streets are safe... old people strut confidently through the darkest alleys... and the weak and nerdy are admired for their computer-programming abilities.
jokes.setup: Homer defending American justice system
jokes.punchline: Obviously false claims about American crime and social conditions
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical speech with escalating absurd claims about American paradise

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Our justice system is the best in the world! Sure, we have a few problems, but name me one country that doesn't have people in it.

jokes.text: Now, as the old saying goes: 'Let your children run wild and free.'
jokes.setup: Homer continuing his parenting philosophy speech
jokes.punchline: Completely made-up 'old saying' that's terrible advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer fabrication of wisdom that justifies his laziness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, there's an old saying: 'Never wear a helmet, it'll mess up your hair.' My dad told me that.

jokes.text: That throwing stick stunt of yours has boomeranged on us.
jokes.setup: Escape situation involving boomerang
jokes.punchline: Literal and metaphorical use of 'boomeranged'
jokes.explanation: Decent pun connecting physical action to metaphorical consequence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A boomerang? Great! I'll throw it and it'll come right back to me.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, that's not how boomerangs work.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, it's like my life choices - they always come back to haunt me!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's actually pretty insightful.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Everything I do boomerangs back on me. My diet, my job, my marriage...

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Hey!

jokes.text: Hey, we can get away in their pouches. It's not like in cartoons. Yeah. There's a lot more mucus.
jokes.setup: Kids trying to escape via kangaroo pouches
jokes.punchline: Grossly realistic details about marsupial biology
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of cartoon logic with disgusting biological reality

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, we're getting in those pouches and bouncing out of here!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, wait. Do you have any idea what you're talking about? Kangaroos don't have pouches like that. The pouch is a fold of skin on the mother's abdomen used to carry underdeveloped joeys. It's lined with mammary glands and has a sphincter muscle that keeps it closed. Plus, it smells like amniotic fluid and kangaroo placenta.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So... that's a no?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's a definitely no.

jokes.text: These bloody things are everywhere. They're in the lift, the lorry... in the bon whizzer, and the Malonga Gilderchuck.
jokes.setup: Complaining about frogs everywhere
jokes.punchline: Mix of real British/Australian terms with completely made-up nonsense
jokes.explanation: Great escalation from real slang to increasingly absurd made-up terms

dialogue.speaker: Australian man
dialogue.line: These bloody frogs are everywhere, mate!

dialogue.speaker: Australian man
dialogue.line: They're hoppin' about like a bunch of crikey-jumpers on a bonzer Tuesday.

dialogue.speaker: Australian man
dialogue.line: I've seen more amphibians than a dingo in a billabong full of snozzwangers!

dialogue.speaker: Australian man
dialogue.line: Need to call the frog-wrangler before they start a foozle uprising!

jokes.text: We have them in America. They're called bullfrogs. What? That's an odd name. I'd have called them chazwazzers.
jokes.setup: Lisa identifying the frogs as bullfrogs
jokes.punchline: Australian man's rejection of normal name for made-up nonsense
jokes.explanation: Excellent made-up Australian slang that became quotable for its pure absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: These are bullfrogs.

dialogue.speaker: Australian man
dialogue.line: Bullfrogs? That's a made-up name! We call 'em hemorrhage toads!

jokes.text: It's time for me to bend over and receive my destiny.
jokes.setup: Bart accepting the booting punishment
jokes.punchline: Overly dramatic phrasing for getting kicked in the butt
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart mix of maturity and inappropriate grandiosity

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, you're gonna kick me in the butt?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's right, boy.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Dad, that's a disproportionate and cruel punishment that violates the Geneva Convention!

jokes.text: Thank you, Bart. I promise I won't make fun of you later for this.
jokes.setup: Bart accepting his punishment with dignity
jokes.punchline: Lisa's promise immediately reveals her intention to mock him later
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa moment - her attempt at kindness immediately undermined by her honest nature and sibling rivalry

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I promise not to mock you while you're being punished.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Thanks, Lis.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But afterwards, all bets are off.

jokes.text: I'm impressed you were able to write so legibly on your own butt.
jokes.setup: Bart showing off his patriotic butt message
jokes.punchline: Lisa's technical appreciation for the practical difficulty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa response - analytical appreciation of Bart's crude gesture

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I have to admit, that's impressive. The muscle control required to articulate individual letters is actually quite remarkable from a physiological standpoint.

jokes.text: No, sir. The closest vessel is the USS Walter Mondale. It's a laundry ship.
jokes.setup: Homer asking about landing on aircraft carrier
jokes.punchline: Ship named after failed presidential candidate that's just for laundry
jokes.explanation: Good political humor combining failed politician with unglamorous military duty

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: So you're telling me we're landing on an aircraft carrier?

dialogue.speaker: Military officer
dialogue.line: That's right. It's the USS Dukakis.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: The USS Dukakis? Named after the failed presidential candidate?

dialogue.speaker: Military officer
dialogue.line: Well, it's just for laundry.

jokes.text: Well, that's what happens when you introduce foreign species... into an ecosystem that can't handle them.
jokes.setup: Seeing the environmental damage from her frog
jokes.punchline: Lisa's scientific explanation of her own mistake
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic ending - Lisa's academic knowledge perfectly explaining her own environmental crime

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh no! My tadpole's eating all the algae, which means the fish have nothing to eat, so they're dying, which means the birds have nothing to eat, so they're dying, and it's all part of the food chain I created!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I've destroyed the entire ecosystem... with science.

tmdb_overview: After tricking an Australian boy into accepting a collect call, Bart flies Down Under to apologize.
insight: Cultural clash sustains 44 jokes across Australia's fish-out-of-water premise.
